content.full_text:
California
Fair Political
Practices Commission
April 13,
1992
Roger W. Krauel
City Attorney
City of Del Mar
c/o Krauel
& Krauel
Governor Park
5090 Shoreham Place,
Suite 101
San Diego,
CA
92122—5934
Re:
Your Request for Informal Assistance
Our File No.
1-92-118
Dear Mr. Krauel:
This is in response to your letter requesting assistance on
behalf of Del Mar City Councilmeinber Jan McMillan with respect to
her responsibilities under the conflict—of—interest provisions of
the Political Reform Act.
1
Since your advice request does not
include facts concerning the nature of the specific decisions in
question or the councilmember’s property interest,
we are treating
your request as one for informal assistance.
2
QUESTION
May Councilmember McMillan participate in decisions
pertaining to the relocation of the public library from a
temporary site within 300 feet of her property, to a permanent
site that is beyond 300 feet,
but within 2,500 feet of her
property?
CONCLUSION
If the decisions concerning the new site for the public
library are interrelated to the use of the site of the temporary
1
Government Code Sections 81000-91015.
All statutory references
are to the Government Code unless otherwise indicated.
Commission
regulations appear at
2 California Code of Regulations Section
18000,
g.
All references to regulations are to Title 2,
Division
6 of the California Code of Regulations.
2
Informal assistance does not provide the requestor with the
immunity provided by an opinion or formal written advice.
(Section
83114; Regulation 18329(c) (3) ..)
428 J Street, Suite 800 • P.O. Box 807 • Sacramento CA 95804-0807 • (916)322-5660


Our File No.
1-92—118
Page
2
library which is within 300 feet of the councilmember’s property,
the councilmember may not participate in the decision if there
will be any financial effect on her real property.
FACTS
Councilmember McMillan owns real property which is within 300
feet of a city—owned lot which contains City Hall,
a parking lot,
a local television studio and a temporary public library.
The city council is currently considering decisions
pertaining to the permanent library site which is slated for the
southeast corner of the city lot,
and is more than 300 feet from
the councilmember’s real property.
You have asked whether the
councilmember may participate in decisions concerning the new
library.
3
ANALYSIS
Conflicts of Interest, Generally
Section 87100 of the Act prohibits any public official from
making, participating in making, or otherwise using his or her
official position to influence a governmental decision which will
have a reasonably foreseeable
4 material financial effect,
distinguishable from its effect on the public generally,
on the
official or a member of his or her immediate family or on any real
property in which the public official has a direct or indirect
interest worth $1,000 or more.
(Section 87103 (b).)
Regulation 18702.3 provides standards to determine whether a
decision will have a material financial effect on an official’s
real property.
Regulation 18702.3(a) provides:
(a)
The effect of a decision is material as
to real property in which an officiaL has a direct,
indirect or beneficial ownership interest (not
including a leasehold interest), if any of the
following applies:
You facts were set out in two groups.
Since it appeared that
both groups dealt with the same question we assumed all the facts
apply equally to both questions. If this is incorrect, please
contact us for further advice.
Whether the financial consequences of a decision are reasonably
foreseeable at the time a governmental decision is made depends on
the facts of each particular case.
An effect is considered
reasonably foreseeable if there is a substantial likelihood that
it will occur.
Certainty is riot required. However, if an effect
is only a mere possibility, it is not reasonably foreseeable. (
re Thorner
(1975)
1 FPPC Ops.
198.)


Our File No.
1-92-118
Page
3
(1)
The real property in which the
official has an interest, or any part of that
real property, is located within a 300 foot
radius of the boundaries
(or the proposed
boundaries)
of the property which is the
subject of the decision, unless the decision
will have no financial effect upon the
official’s real property interest.
(3)
The real property in which the
official has an interest is located outside a
radius of 300 feet and any part of the real
property is located within a radius of 2,500
feet of the boundaries (or the proposed
boundaries) of the property which is the
subject of the decision and the decision will
have a reasonably foreseeable financial effect
of:
(A)
Ten thousand dollars
($10,000)
or more on the fair market value of the
real property in which the official has
an interest; or
(B)
Will affect the rental value of
the property by $1,000 or more per 12
month period.
Regulation 18702.3(a) (1).
You stated that Councilmember McMillan owns real property
that is within 300 feet of a city-owned lot which contains City
Hall,
a parking 1t,
a local television studio and a temporary
public library.
The city council is currently considering a
project for the southeast corner of the lot which is more than 300
feet from the councilmember’s real property.
Generally,
each governmental decision is analyzed
independently to determine if there will be a foreseeable material
financial effect on an official’s financial interest.
(In re Owen
(1976)
2
FPPC Ops.
77..)
According to Regulation 18702.3, the
distance between the subject property and the councilmember’s
property determines which test applies.
Thus, if the city council is considering the construction of
a library on a site which is more than 300 feet, but less than
2,500 feet from the nearest boundary of the councilmember’s
property,
Councilmember McMillan may participate so long as the
decision will not have a $10,000 or greater effect on the fair
market value of her real property, or will affect the rental value
of the property by $1,000 or more per 12-month period. (
generally, Hurst Advice Letter,
No. 1-90-359.)
Conversely, to the


Our File No.
1—92—118
Page 4
extent that the property which is the subject of the library
decision is the entire lot,
including city hail and the cable
television station, the subject property would be within 300 feet
and the councilmember would not be able to participate in the
decision if there was any financial effect on his property.
It appears from your facts that the subject of the decision
is limited to an area beyond 300 feet of the councilmember’s
property.
We assume that the council’s deliberation will be
limited to this area and that the boundaries have been set by
application or some other documented action of the city.
To the
extent that the more distant boundaries are the boundaries of the
subject property, the boundary around the library site would be
the appropriate boundary to measure from and the test in
Regulation 18702.3(a) (3)
would apply.
However,
even if the there will not be a $10,000 affect on
the councilmember’s real property, the councilmember may still
have to disqualify himself from the decision.
Under some
circumstances a series of decisions may be too interrelated to be
considered separately.
(Miller Advice Letter,
No. A-82-119.)
In
this case the decisions concerning the project at the more distant
location and the use of the property within 300 feet of the
councilmember’s property are interlinked since the permanent
library decision will decide or alter the
decision concerning the
use of the nearer property as the temporary library.
Thus, the
councilmember would be disqualified as to both decisions if there
is any financial effect on her property.
(Nord Advice Letter,
No.
A—82—038.)
However, once the decision to locate the permanent library on
the more distant site is made, it would not appear that future
implementation decisions concerning the new library will affect
the use of the old site.
Assuming that the implementation
decisions are not interrelated to the decisions concerning the old
site, the decisions would be analyzed independently to determine
if the councilmember will still be required to disqualify herself
from the decisions.
As discussed above, since the new site is
more than 300 feet from the councilmember’s property, but within
2,500 feet, Regulation 18702.3(a) (3)
would be the applicable
provision.
Public Generally
The Act also provides that public officials with interests
that will be financially affected by a decision may participate if
the effect on their interests is not distinguishable from the
effect on the public generally.
(Regulation 18703.)
For the
“public generally” exception to apply,
a decision must affect the
official’s interests in substantially the same manner as it would
affect a significant segment of the population of the
jurisdiction.
(In re Owen (l76)
2 FPPC Ops.
77.)


Our File No.
1—92—118
Page 5
We have not discussed application of the exception to your
situation because we do not have sufficient facts to reach a
conclusion.
It would appear, however, that financial effects on
the councilmember’s property due to its close proximity to the old
library site would not be similar to the effect of the decision on
a significant segment of Del Mar.
If you have any further questions regarding this matter,
please feel free to contact me at
(916)
322—59Ol.
Sincerely,
SH:JWW:aa
Scott Hallabrin
General Counsel
John W. Wallace
Counsel, Legal Division
Copies of Commission regulations and Opinions are available in
many law libraries.
Alternatively, copies of these materials and
Commission advice letters may be obtained from the Commission at a
cost of 1Oc per page.
